judge_output.rationale: The agent recovered four of five expected required findings: Jacob, Thomas, William, and Josephus Geach (f1, f3, f4, and an implicit recovery of Josephus in the proof but not explicitly listed in the findings) are all properly established as children of Peter and Rebecca Geach in the final tree with correct birth dates and parent relationships. Of the five required findings, only three are fully matched (f1, f3, f4), one is partially matched (f2—Mary is in the tree but not as a sibling), and one is completely missed (f5—Nancy). The recall ratio for required findings is 3.5 out of 5 = 0.70 on strict counting, or 0.80 if crediting partial. The verdict is partial rather than pass because at least one required finding (Nancy Geach, f5) was not recovered at all.

proof_quality.rationale: The agent conducted an exhaustive search across 23 FamilySearch log entries covering multiple record types (Find-A-Grave, federal censuses 1860–1880, Ohio county death records, Licking County deeds and marriages, probate indexes). The narrative tier ('Probable') is appropriately justified by convergent circumstantial evidence: rare surname concentration, birth-year clustering within the Peter-Rebecca childbearing window, shared Licking County geography, shared burial community at Maple Grove Cemetery, and absence of competing Geach families. Evidence comes from independent record types (censuses, Find-A-Grave, county records, deeds). However, the proof rests on absence of direct parentage documents (no probate petition or vital record naming the siblings as children of Peter) rather than positive confirmation. The agent did not find or cite the 1836 probate petition that the expected findings reference as the key supporting source—this is a notable oversight that would have provided direct documentary evidence. Corroboration is strong across multiple independent sources. The score is 2 (partial) rather than 3 because the agent's proof does not cite the most definitive source (the probate petition itself), instead building an inference from census and burial data alone, and because the proof summary conspicuously omits Nancy and Mary, suggesting incomplete source analysis relative to the task's full requirements.

entry.rationale: The 1850 federal census names every household member with age and birthplace. Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach, widowed since 1836, would be head of household at age ~49; any children born after ~1822 and still unmarried would appear. Elizabeth (b. 1822) had already married Jonathan Slack by 1844 and would not be in Rebecca's household, but younger siblings would be. This is the highest-yield single source for identifying younger Geach siblings.

Peter Geach died April 1836. His estate administration or will in the Licking County Probate Court would name all legal heirs — typically all surviving children. This is the single most authoritative source for identifying every sibling. FamilySearch collection 'Ohio, Probate Records, 1789–1996' (coll. 1992421) holds Licking County probate images. Courthouse fire noted for 1867–1872 burnt records should not affect an 1836 filing.

Peter died April 1836, so Rebecca heads the household by 1840. The 1840 census lists the household by age/sex tally (not individual names), but it will show how many females under age 20 and males of various ages were still in the household — corroborating the number of children identified from other sources. FamilySearch collection 'United States Census, 1840' covers Licking County.

Peter Geach is head of household in 1830. The 1830 census shows age/sex tallies only, but will reveal the number and approximate ages of children present — including any children born before Elizabeth (b. 1822) who would appear as females age 5–10 or older. Comparing 1820 and 1830 household tallies helps reconstruct the birth order. FamilySearch collection 'United States Census, 1830'.

County marriage records for Licking County are indexed on FamilySearch (collection 1614804, Ohio County Marriages 1789–2016). Any daughters of Peter and Rebecca would appear under the Geach surname upon marriage. Sons would appear as groom. Searching 'Geach' in Licking County 1818–1870 should surface all marriage events for siblings of Elizabeth, including the parents' own 1818 marriage already in the tree.

Licking County partition records (court-ordered land divisions among heirs) from 1816–1869 are digitized on FamilySearch as fulltext-searchable browse volumes. The 1831–1844 volume (imageGroup 005870804_002) covers the period immediately after Peter's death and would capture any partition of his real property among his heirs. Heirs named in partition proceedings = siblings of Elizabeth. Also check 1845–1851 and later volumes for any delayed partitions.

The Geach family buried multiple members at Maple Grove Cemetery in Granville. FamilySearch has digitized Granville Township parish records 1831–1848 (imageGroup 008166808_001, 118 images, browse-only). A church membership or baptism register from this congregation could name Geach children by birth year. This is the primary church-record item; broader Ohio Church Records collection (coll. 2787827) is a supplementary fallback.

FamilySearch collection 'Ohio, County Death Records, 1840–2001' (coll. 2128172) indexes Licking County deaths. Any sibling of Elizabeth who died in Licking County after 1840 would appear here with parents' names (if recorded), enabling identification and cross-referencing. This also catches siblings who predeceased Rebecca. Searching surname 'Geach' in Licking County is a short, high-specificity search.

**Open question:** q_001 — "What children of Peter Geach and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach of Union Township, Licking County, Ohio are identified in census, probate, and vital records from 1820 to 1870?"

**Context:** This is the 1870 US Census record for "Jacob Geuch" (indexed spelling variant of Geach), head of household in Union Township, Licking County, Ohio. Birth year given as 1813 (known age inflation — other records give b.~1820). Jacob Geach is already in the project tree as person I4. His wife is indexed as "Mary Geuch" (b.1824, Ohio). ## Probable Identification of the Siblings of Elizabeth Geach

**Research Question:** What children of Peter Geach and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach of Union Township, Licking County, Ohio are identified in census, probate, and vital records from 1820 to 1870?

**Conclusion (Probable):** Jacob Geach (b. ~1820), Thomas Geach (b. 17 Mar 1827), William W. Geach Sr. (b. 14 May 1830), and Josephus Geach (b. 18 Dec 1831) are *probably* siblings of Elizabeth Geach, all children of Peter Geach (d. Apr 1836, Licking County, Ohio) and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach (d. 31 Aug 1870, Granville, Licking County, Ohio). No document directly names any of these men as a child of Peter and Rebecca; the conclusion rests on convergent circumstantial evidence from multiple independent record types.

### Family Context

Peter Geach and Rebecca Benjamin married in Licking County, Ohio on 29 September 1818. Peter died in April 1836 in Licking County. Rebecca survived until 31 August 1870, residing in Licking County through the year of her death — she appears as a household member in the George Shull household in the June 1870 census (src_010) and receives a deed from Christopher Benjamin (possibly her brother) between 1836 and 1848 (src_013). Their daughter Elizabeth Geach was born 17 August 1822 (273D-F9Z). This places the couple's childbearing window from approximately 1819 to April 1836.

### Evidence for Each Probable Sibling

**Jacob Geach (I4; b. ~1820, Ohio).** The 1870 federal census for Union Township, Licking County lists 'Jacob Geuch' (surname variant) as head of household, age ~50, born Ohio (src_012). The 1880 census confirms Jacob Geach in the same county with wife Mary and children (src_006). A Licking County death record documents Jacob's death (src_004). His 1847 marriage to Mary Grubb in Licking County is documented in Ohio county marriage records (src_015). Jacob's birth year of ~1820 places him about two years before Elizabeth (b. 1822), consistent with an elder sibling.

**Thomas Geach (I1; b. 17 Mar 1827, d. 27 Dec 1897).** A Find-A-Grave memorial confirms his birth date and burial at Maple Grove Cemetery, Granville, Licking County, Ohio (src_001). The 1870 census lists Thomas Geach as head of household in Granville Township (src_009). The 1880 census confirms the same household (src_005). A speculative link associates 'T.S. Geach', surveyor in an 1846 Licking County property partition (src_014), with Thomas, though this rests on initials only. Thomas's birth year of 1827 falls within the Peter-Rebecca childbearing window.

**William W. Geach Sr. (I2; b. 14 May 1830, d. 2 Jul 1908).** A Find-A-Grave memorial confirms his birth date and burial at Maple Grove Cemetery, Granville, Licking County — the same cemetery as Thomas Geach (src_002). The 1870 census lists William Geach as head of household in Granville Township (src_011). The 1880 census confirms this household (src_008). William's birth year of 1830 is six years before Peter's death.

**Josephus Geach (I3; b. 18 Dec 1831, d. 12 Aug 1906).** A Find-A-Grave memorial confirms his birth date (src_003); he is buried in Republic, Marquette County, Michigan, having emigrated from Ohio. The 1860 federal census for Granville Village, Licking County, lists 'J Geach', male, b. ~1831, Ohio, as head of household (src_007) — his last documented presence in Licking County before migration to Michigan. Josephus's birth year of 1831 is five years before Peter's death.

### Correlation and Analysis

Five independent lines of evidence converge:

1. **Rare surname.** 'Geach' is an uncommon surname of Cornish-British origin. All searches of FamilySearch census, vital, deed, and full-text records for Licking County found no Geach family other than Peter-Rebecca's known household and these four adult men. The concentration of an unusual surname in a single county strongly implies a shared progenitor.

2. **Birth years fill the family window exactly.** Peter and Rebecca married September 1818; Peter died April 1836. The five children's birth years — Jacob (~1820), Elizabeth (1822), Thomas (1827), William (1830), Josephus (1831) — span 1820–1831 and fit naturally within an 18-year family window ending at Peter's death. No individual falls outside this range.

3. **Geographic concentration.** Jacob, Thomas, and William all resided in Licking County (Granville Township and Union Township), and Josephus resided in Granville Village through at least 1860. This is the same county where Peter and Rebecca lived.

4. **Shared burial community.** Thomas and William are buried at Maple Grove Cemetery, Granville — the same cemetery as Elizabeth Geach Slack. A shared family burial ground in the same township reinforces common origin.

5. **No competing Geach family found.** The exhaustive FamilySearch search (23 log entries, 15 sources, multiple record types) produced no evidence of any other Geach family in Licking County. The absence of any competing Geach household eliminates alternative explanations for the surname cluster.

### Negative Evidence

No probate record of Peter Geach naming his heirs was found in any FamilySearch collection. Early Licking County probate records accessible via FTS are partially transcribed index images with heavy OCR noise; a Geach entry cannot be confirmed or denied from this source. Rebecca's household in the 1840 and 1850 censuses was not identified in indexed FamilySearch records. These gaps mean the conclusion cannot be elevated to 'Proved.'

### Conclusion

On the basis of convergent circumstantial evidence — rare surname concentration, birth years fitting the Peter-Rebecca childbearing window, shared Licking County geography, shared burial community, and absence of any competing Geach family — Jacob Geach, Thomas Geach, William W. Geach Sr., and Josephus Geach are **probably** children of Peter Geach and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach and therefore siblings of Elizabeth Geach. This conclusion meets the GPS tier of *Probable*: multiple independent record types support the identification; no conflicting evidence was found; but direct parentage evidence (a will, deed of partition, or contemporaneous family record naming these individuals as Peter and Rebecca's children) is absent.

fields.narrative_markdown:
## Probable Identification of the Siblings of Elizabeth Geach

**Research Question:** What children of Peter Geach and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach of Union Township, Licking County, Ohio are identified in census, probate, and vital records from 1820 to 1870?

**Conclusion (Probable):** Jacob Geach (b. ~1820), Thomas Geach (b. 17 Mar 1827), William W. Geach Sr. (b. 14 May 1830), and Josephus Geach (b. 18 Dec 1831) are *probably* siblings of Elizabeth Geach — all children of Peter Geach (d. Apr 1836, Licking County, Ohio) and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach (d. 31 Aug 1870, Granville, Licking County, Ohio). No document directly names any of these men as a child of Peter and Rebecca; the conclusion rests on convergent circumstantial evidence from multiple independent record types.

### Family Context

Peter Geach and Rebecca Benjamin married in Licking County, Ohio on 29 September 1818. Peter died in April 1836. Rebecca died 31 August 1870, residing in Licking County through her death — enumerated in the June 1870 census as a household member in the Shull household (src_010) and named as grantee in a deed from Christopher Benjamin between 1836 and 1848 (src_013). Their daughter Elizabeth Geach was born 17 August 1822 (273D-F9Z). This places the couple's childbearing window from approximately 1819 to April 1836.

### Evidence for Each Probable Sibling

**Jacob Geach (I4; b. ~1820, Ohio).** The 1870 federal census for Union Township, Licking County lists 'Jacob Geuch' (surname variant) as head of household, age ~50, born Ohio (src_012). The 1880 census confirms Jacob Geach in the same county with wife Mary and children (src_006). A Licking County death record documents Jacob's death (src_004). His 1847 marriage to Mary Grubb in Licking County is confirmed in Ohio county marriage records (src_015). Jacob's birth year of ~1820 places him about two years before Elizabeth (b. 1822).

**Thomas Geach (I1; b. 17 Mar 1827, d. 27 Dec 1897).** A Find-A-Grave memorial confirms his birth date and burial at Maple Grove Cemetery, Granville, Licking County (src_001). The 1870 census lists Thomas Geach as head of household in Granville Township, Licking County (src_009). The 1880 census confirms this household (src_005). A speculative link to 'T.S. Geach', surveyor in an 1846 Licking County property partition (src_014), is noted but rests on initials only.

**William W. Geach Sr. (I2; b. 14 May 1830, d. 2 Jul 1908).** A Find-A-Grave memorial confirms his birth date and burial at Maple Grove Cemetery, Granville, Licking County — the same cemetery as Thomas Geach and Elizabeth Geach Slack (src_002). The 1870 census lists William Geach as head of household in Granville Township (src_011). The 1880 census confirms this household (src_008).

**Josephus Geach (I3; b. 18 Dec 1831, d. 12 Aug 1906).** A Find-A-Grave memorial confirms his birth date; he died in Republic, Marquette County, Michigan, having emigrated from Ohio (src_003). The 1860 federal census for Granville Village, Licking County, lists 'J Geach', male, b. ~1831, Ohio, as head of household (src_007) — his last documented Licking County residence before migration.

### Correlation and Analysis

Five independent lines of evidence converge:

1. **Rare surname.** 'Geach' is an uncommon surname of Cornish-British origin. All searches found no Geach family in Licking County other than Peter-Rebecca's known household and these four adult men. Concentration of an unusual surname in a single county strongly implies a shared progenitor.

2. **Birth years fill the family window exactly.** Peter and Rebecca married September 1818; Peter died April 1836. The five children's birth years — Jacob (~1820), Elizabeth (1822), Thomas (1827), William (1830), Josephus (1831) — span 1820–1831 and fit naturally within the 18-year family window ending at Peter's death.

3. **Geographic concentration.** Jacob, Thomas, and William resided in Licking County (Granville Township and Union Township); Josephus resided in Granville Village through at least 1860. This is the same county where Peter and Rebecca lived and where Elizabeth was born.

4. **Shared burial community.** Thomas and William are buried at Maple Grove Cemetery, Granville — the same cemetery as Elizabeth Geach Slack. A shared family burial ground in the same township reinforces common origin.

5. **No competing Geach family found; nephew/cousin alternative ruled out.** Exhaustive FamilySearch searching (23 log entries, 15 sources, multiple record types) produced no evidence of any other Geach family in Licking County. The birth spacing across five children over eleven years (Jacob ~1820 through Josephus 1831) is consistent with a single nuclear family. No adult male Geach other than these four individuals appears in any Licking County record, ruling out the alternative that they are nephews or cousins of Peter rather than his sons: there is simply no other Geach man in the county who could have been their father.

### Negative Evidence and Candidate Elimination

No probate record of Peter Geach naming his heirs was found. Early Licking County probate records accessible via FTS are partially transcribed index images with heavy OCR noise; a Geach entry cannot be confirmed or denied. Rebecca's household in the 1840 and 1850 censuses was not identified in indexed FamilySearch records. These gaps prevent elevation of the conclusion to 'Proved.'

A fifth candidate, Mary Eleanor Geach (b. 29 Mar 1825, d. 6 Dec 1905, bur. Granville), was initially noted as a plausible sibling based on her birth year and Granville burial location. However, her Ohio county death record (ark:/61903/1:1:F6VT-P45, 'Ohio, County Death Records, 1840-2001') names her parents as Samuel Grubbs, born Virginia, and Margaret Windsor, born Virginia — not Peter Geach and Rebecca Benjamin of Ohio. Mary Eleanor bears the surname Geach by marriage, not by birth. She is therefore excluded from the sibling group.

### Conclusion

On the basis of convergent circumstantial evidence — rare surname concentration, birth years fitting the Peter-Rebecca childbearing window, shared Licking County geography, shared burial community, and absence of any competing Geach family or alternative paternal Geach line — Jacob Geach (I4), Thomas Geach (I1), William W. Geach Sr. (I2), and Josephus Geach (I3) are **probably** children of Peter Geach and Rebecca (Benjamin) Geach and therefore siblings of Elizabeth Geach (273D-F9Z). GPS tier: *Probable* — multiple independent record types support the identification; no conflicting evidence found; direct parentage evidence absent.

### Sources Cited

- **src_009**: Household of Thomas Geach, 'United States, Census, 1870,' FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:MHXL-CZV).
- **src_010**: Rebecca Geach in Shull household, 'United States Census, 1870,' FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M6VT-WXV).
- **src_011**: Household of William Geach, 'United States, Census, 1870,' FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M62Y-2P2).
- **src_012**: Household of Jacob Geuch, 'United States, Census, 1870,' FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M6VT-W6N).
- **src_013**: Licking County deed index 1848, Rebecca Geach grantee from Christopher Benjamin, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:3Q9M-CS4T-L3VL-M).
- **src_014**: Licking County property partition 1846, T.S. Geach surveyor, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:3QSQ-G9QG-LG67).
